content:
# Exodus Chapter 27 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~48r–48v (PDF pp. 95–96); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 69–70 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_027_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_027_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_027_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_027_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE OUTER PRECINCTS:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 27 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter moves outward from the tabernacle structure of chapter 26 to describe the outer precincts: the bronze altar for burnt offerings (vv. 1–8), the linen-walled courtyard enclosure (vv. 9–19), and the perpetual oil for the lampstand (vv. 20–21). The movement is architecturally logical — having described the dwelling itself, the text now addresses the open-air precinct surrounding it. The bronze altar is the first object encountered upon entering the courtyard, the place where Israel's sacrificial worship is enacted in public space. The courtyard hangings create the boundary between sacred and common ground. The chapter closes with the oil commandment (vv. 20–21), which shifts from architecture to perpetual liturgical practice — the first ongoing obligation specified in the tabernacle instructions. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 69–70) presents the LXX in continuous uncial script; the LXX versification aligns with the MT throughout this chapter.

---

## The Bronze Altar (vv. 1–8)

**1** And you shall make [ve-asita] the altar [et-ha-mizbeakh] of acacia [atsei shittim] wood: five [khamesh] cubits [ammot] the length [orekh] and five [ve-khamesh] cubits [ammot] the width [rokhav]; the altar [ha-mizbeakh] shall be [yihyeh] square [ravua], and three [ve-shalosh] cubits [ammot] its height [qomato].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָשִׂיתָ אֶת־הַמִּזְבֵּחַ עֲצֵי שִׁטִּים חָמֵשׁ אַמּוֹת אֹרֶךְ וְחָמֵשׁ אַמּוֹת רֹחַב רָבוּעַ יִהְיֶה הַמִּזְבֵּחַ וְשָׁלֹשׁ אַמּוֹת קֹמָתוֹ. **Key terms:** מִזְבֵּחַ (mizbeakh) = "altar" — from זבח (zavakh, "to slaughter, sacrifice"); literally "place of slaughter." רָבוּעַ (ravua) = "square, fourfold" — qal passive participle of רבע (rava, "to make square"); the altar is a perfect square in plan, 5 x 5 cubits (approximately 7.5 x 7.5 feet), standing 3 cubits high (approximately 4.5 feet). קֹמָתוֹ (qomato) = "its height" — from קומה (qomah, "stature, height"). LXX: καὶ ποιήσεις θυσιαστήριον ἐκ ξύλων ἀσήπτων πέντε πήχεων τὸ μῆκος καὶ πέντε πήχεων τὸ εὖρος τετράγωνον ἔσται τὸ θυσιαστήριον καὶ τριῶν πήχεων τὸ ὕψος αὐτοῦ — θυσιαστήριον ("altar, place of sacrifice") for mizbeakh; τετράγωνος ("four-cornered, square") for ravua.

**2** And you shall make [ve-asita] its horns [qarnotav] upon [al] its four [arba] corners [pinnotav]; from it [mimmenu] its horns [qarnotav] shall be [tihyeyna], and you shall overlay [ve-tsippita] it [oto] with bronze [nekhoshet].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָשִׂיתָ קַרְנֹתָיו עַל אַרְבַּע פִּנֹּתָיו מִמֶּנּוּ תִּהְיֶיןָ קַרְנֹתָיו וְצִפִּיתָ אֹתוֹ נְחֹשֶׁת. **Key terms:** קַרְנֹתָיו (qarnotav) = "its horns" — from קרן (qeren, "horn"); projecting horn-shaped extensions at each corner of the altar. These are not separate attachments but integral to the structure — מִמֶּנּוּ (mimmenu) = "from it," meaning the horns are of one piece with the altar itself. The horns serve as the point of blood application (Lev 4:7, 18, 25, 30) and as a place of asylum (1 Kgs 1:50, 2:28). פִּנֹּתָיו (pinnotav) = "its corners" — from פנה (pinnah, "corner"). LXX: καὶ ποιήσεις τὰ κέρατα ἐπὶ τῶν τεσσάρων γωνιῶν ἐξ αὐτοῦ ἔσται τὰ κέρατα — κέρας ("horn") for qeren.

**3** And you shall make [ve-asita] its pots [sirotav] for removing its ashes [le-dashno], and its shovels [ve-ya'av], and its basins [u-mizraqotav], and its forks [u-mizlegotav], and its firepans [u-makhtotav]; for all [le-khol] its vessels [kelav] you shall make [ta'aseh] of bronze [nekhoshet].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָשִׂיתָ סִירֹתָיו לְדַשְּׁנוֹ וְיָעָיו וּמִזְרְקֹתָיו וּמִזְלְגֹתָיו וּמַחְתֹּתָיו לְכָל־כֵּלָיו תַּעֲשֶׂה נְחֹשֶׁת. **Key terms:** סִירֹתָיו (sirotav) = "its pots" — from סיר (sir, "pot, cauldron"); vessels for collecting ash. לְדַשְּׁנוֹ (le-dashno) = "for removing its ashes" — piel infinitive construct of דשׁן (dashan, "to remove fat-ashes") + 3ms suffix; the fatty ash residue from burnt offerings. יָעָיו (ya'av) = "its shovels" — from יעה (ya'ah, "to sweep, shovel"); flat scoops for handling ash. מִזְרְקֹתָיו (mizraqotav) = "its basins" — from זרק (zaraq, "to toss, sprinkle"); basins for catching and tossing sacrificial blood against the altar. מִזְלְגֹתָיו (mizlegotav) = "its forks" — from a root related to "prong"; three-pronged forks for handling sacrificial flesh (cf. 1 Sam 2:13). מַחְתֹּתָיו (makhtotav) = "its firepans" — from חתה (khatah, "to snatch fire, take coals"); censers or fire-carrying pans. LXX: καὶ ποιήσεις στεφάνην τῷ θυσιαστηρίῳ... — the LXX here diverges in order, inserting the "rim" (στεφάνη) before the utensils, and renders the utensils list differently.

**4** And you shall make [ve-asita] for it [lo] a grating [mikhbar], a network [ma'aseh] of bronze [nekhoshet reshet], and you shall make [ve-asita] upon [al] the net [ha-reshet] four [arba] rings [tabb'ot] of bronze [nekhoshet] upon [al] its four [arba] ends [qetsotav].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָשִׂיתָ לוֹ מִכְבָּר מַעֲשֵׂה רֶשֶׁת נְחֹשֶׁת וְעָשִׂיתָ עַל־הָרֶשֶׁת אַרְבַּע טַבְּעֹת נְחֹשֶׁת עַל אַרְבַּע קְצוֹתָיו. **Key terms:** מִכְבָּר (mikhbar) = "grating" — from כבר (kavar, "to interweave, multiply"); an interwoven mesh or lattice. רֶשֶׁת (reshet) = "net, network" — a bronze mesh grating, likely positioned partway down inside the altar to support the fire and allow ash to fall through, or fitted around the lower exterior. The exact placement is debated. LXX: καὶ ποιήσεις αὐτῷ ἐσχάραν ἔργῳ δικτυωτῷ χαλκῆν — ἐσχάρα ("hearth, grating") for mikhbar; δικτυωτός ("net-like") for reshet.

**5** And you shall put [ve-natattah] it [otah] under [takhat] the ledge [karkob] of the altar [ha-mizbeakh], from below [mi-lmattah]; and the net [ve-haytah ha-reshet] shall be [ad] unto the middle [khatsi] of the altar [ha-mizbeakh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְנָתַתָּה אֹתָהּ תַּחַת הַכַּרְכֹּב הַמִּזְבֵּחַ מִלְּמָטָּה וְהָיְתָה הָרֶשֶׁת עַד חֲצִי הַמִּזְבֵּחַ. **Key terms:** כַּרְכֹּב (karkob) = "ledge, rim, surrounding border" — a decorative or structural rim around the altar; the exact form is debated, but it appears to be a projecting ledge at or below the midpoint. The net extends from below this ledge down to the midpoint of the altar's height. LXX: καὶ ὑποθήσεις αὐτοὺς ὑπὸ τὴν ἐσχάραν τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου κάτωθεν ἔσται δὲ ἡ ἐσχάρα ἕως τοῦ ἡμίσους τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου — ἥμισυς ("half") for khatsi.

**6** And you shall make [ve-asita] poles [baddim] for the altar [la-mizbeakh], poles [baddei] of acacia [atsei shittim] wood, and you shall overlay [ve-tsippita] them [otam] with bronze [nekhoshet].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָשִׂיתָ בַדִּים לַמִּזְבֵּחַ בַּדֵּי עֲצֵי שִׁטִּים וְצִפִּיתָ אֹתָם נְחֹשֶׁת. **Key terms:** בַדִּים (baddim) = "poles, carrying-bars" — from בד (bad, "part, limb, pole"); the same term used for the ark's poles (25:13–15) and the table's poles (25:28), but here overlaid with bronze rather than gold — consistent with the material hierarchy: gold for inner furnishings, bronze for the courtyard altar. LXX: καὶ ποιήσεις τῷ θυσιαστηρίῳ φορεῖς ἐκ ξύλων ἀσήπτων καὶ περιχαλκώσεις αὐτούς — φορεύς ("carrier, pole") for bad; περιχαλκόω ("overlay with bronze") for ve-tsippita nekhoshet.

**7** And the poles [ve-huvah et-baddav] shall be brought into [ba-tabb'ot] the rings, and the poles [ve-hayu ha-baddim] shall be [al] upon the two [shnei] sides [tsalot] of the altar [ha-mizbeakh] when carrying [bi-s'et] it [oto].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהוּבָא אֶת־בַּדָּיו בַּטַּבָּעֹת וְהָיוּ הַבַּדִּים עַל־שְׁנֵי צַלְעֹת הַמִּזְבֵּחַ בִּשְׂאֵת אֹתוֹ. **Key terms:** וְהוּבָא (ve-huvah) = hophal perfect of בוא (bo, "to come, bring") — "and it/they shall be brought"; the poles are inserted into the rings. בִּשְׂאֵת (bi-s'et) = qal infinitive construct of נשׂא (nasa, "to lift, carry") + preposition — "when carrying, when lifting"; the poles are for transporting the altar during Israel's wilderness journeys. LXX: καὶ εἰσάξεις τοὺς φορεῖς εἰς τοὺς δακτυλίους — εἰσάγω ("bring in") for ve-huvah.

**8** Hollow [nevuv] of boards [lukhot] you shall make [ta'aseh] it [oto]; as [ka'asher] he showed [her'ah] you [otekha] on the mountain [ba-har], so [ken] they shall make [ya'asu] it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads נְבוּב לֻחֹת תַּעֲשֶׂה אֹתוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר הֶרְאָה אֹתְךָ בָּהָר כֵּן יַעֲשׂוּ. **Key terms:** נְבוּב (nevuv) = "hollow" — qal passive participle of נבב (navav, "to hollow out"); the acacia-wood altar is not solid but hollow, presumably filled with earth when set up (cf. 20:24). לֻחֹת (lukhot) = "boards, planks" — from לוח (luakh, "board, tablet"); the same word used for the tablets of the covenant (24:12), but here simply denoting flat planks forming the hollow box. כַּאֲשֶׁר הֶרְאָה אֹתְךָ בָּהָר (ka'asher her'ah otekha ba-har) = "as he showed you on the mountain" — hiphil perfect of ראה (ra'ah, "to see"); echoing 25:9, 40 and 26:30, reaffirming the heavenly pattern. LXX: κοῖλον σανιδωτὸν ποιήσεις αὐτό κατὰ τὸ παραδειχθέν σοι ἐν τῷ ὄρει — κοῖλος ("hollow") for nevuv; σανιδωτός ("of planks") for lukhot.

---

## The Courtyard of the Tabernacle (vv. 9–19)

**9** And you shall make [ve-asita] the courtyard [et-khatsar] of the tabernacle [ha-mishkan]: for the south [negev] side [li-fe'at], southward [teimanah], hangings [qela'im] for the courtyard [le-khatser] of fine twisted linen [shesh moshzar], a hundred [me'ah] cubits [va-ammah] long [orekh] for the one [ha-ekhad] side [la-pe'ah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעָשִׂיתָ אֵת חֲצַר הַמִּשְׁכָּן לִפְאַת נֶגֶב־תֵּימָנָה קְלָעִים לֶחָצֵר שֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר מֵאָה בָאַמָּה אֹרֶךְ לַפֵּאָה הָאֶחָת. **Key terms:** חֲצַר (khatsar) = "courtyard, enclosed area" — from חצר (khatsar, "to enclose"); the open-air precinct surrounding the tabernacle. קְלָעִים (qela'im) = "hangings, curtains" — from קלע (qala, "to sling, hang"); linen fabric panels hung between the courtyard pillars. The south side is 100 cubits long (approximately 150 feet). LXX: καὶ ποιήσεις αὐλὴν τῇ σκηνῇ εἰς τὸ κλίτος τὸ πρὸς λίβα ἱστία τῆς αὐλῆς ἐκ βύσσου κεκλωσμένης — αὐλή ("courtyard") for khatsar; ἱστίον ("sail, curtain") for qela.

**10** And its pillars [ve-ammudav] twenty [esrim], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] twenty [esrim] of bronze [nekhoshet]; the hooks [vavei] of the pillars [ha-ammudim] and their bands [va-khashaqeihem] of silver [khesef].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְעַמּוּדָיו עֶשְׂרִים וְאַדְנֵיהֶם עֶשְׂרִים נְחֹשֶׁת וָוֵי הָעַמּוּדִים וַחֲשֻׁקֵיהֶם כָּסֶף. **Key terms:** חֲשֻׁקֵיהֶם (khashaqeihem) = "their bands, their fillets" — from חשׁק (khashaq, "to bind, attach, desire"); decorative silver bands or connecting rods encircling the pillars near the top. The material combination for the courtyard pillars: bronze sockets (foundations), silver hooks and bands (attachments), continuing the material hierarchy — bronze at ground level, silver for connectors. LXX: καὶ οἱ στῦλοι αὐτῆς εἴκοσι καὶ αἱ βάσεις αὐτῶν εἴκοσι χαλκαῖ — the LXX omits specific mention of the hooks and bands here.

**11** And likewise [ve-khen] for the north [tsafon] side [li-fe'at] in length [ba-orekh], hangings [qela'im] of a hundred [me'ah] long [orekh], and its pillars [ve-ammudav] twenty [esrim], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] twenty [esrim] of bronze [nekhoshet]; the hooks [vavei] of the pillars [ha-ammudim] and their bands [va-khashaqeihem] of silver [khesef].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְכֵן לִפְאַת צָפוֹן בָּאֹרֶךְ קְלָעִים מֵאָה אֹרֶךְ וְעַמֻּדָיו עֶשְׂרִים וְאַדְנֵיהֶם עֶשְׂרִים נְחֹשֶׁת וָוֵי הָעַמּוּדִים וַחֲשֻׁקֵיהֶם כָּסֶף. The north side mirrors the south side exactly: 100 cubits of hangings, twenty pillars, twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and bands.

**12** And the width [ve-rokhav] of the courtyard [ha-khatser] on the west [yam] side [li-fe'at]: hangings [qela'im] of fifty [khamishim] cubits [ammah]; their pillars [ammudeihem] ten [asarah], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] ten [asarah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְרֹחַב הֶחָצֵר לִפְאַת־יָם קְלָעִים חֲמִשִּׁים אַמָּה עַמֻּדֵיהֶם עֲשָׂרָה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם עֲשָׂרָה. **Key terms:** The west side (rear) is 50 cubits (approximately 75 feet), making the courtyard a 100 x 50 cubit rectangle. יָם (yam) = "sea" = "west" — the same directional convention as 26:22. LXX: καὶ τὸ εὖρος τῆς αὐλῆς τὸ πρὸς θάλασσαν ἱστία πεντήκοντα πήχεων — consistent rendering.

**13** And the width [ve-rokhav] of the courtyard [ha-khatser] on the east [qedmah] side [li-fe'at], eastward [mizrakhah]: fifty [khamishim] cubits [ammah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְרֹחַב הֶחָצֵר לִפְאַת קֵדְמָה מִזְרָחָה חֲמִשִּׁים אַמָּה. **Key terms:** קֵדְמָה מִזְרָחָה (qedmah mizrakhah) = "eastward, toward the sunrise" — a double directional marker like negbah teimanah (v. 9): qedem = "front, east" (from qadam, "to be in front") and mizrakh = "place of rising" (from zarakh, "to rise, shine"). The east side faces the entrance and is also 50 cubits total, but divided into sections (vv. 14–16). LXX: καὶ τὸ εὖρος τῆς αὐλῆς τὸ πρὸς ἀνατολάς πεντήκοντα πήχεων — ἀνατολή ("rising, east") for mizrakhah.

**14** And fifteen [khamesh esreh] cubits [ammah] of hangings [qela'im] for the one shoulder [la-katef]; their pillars [ammudeihem] three [sheloshah], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] three [sheloshah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַחֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה אַמָּה קְלָעִים לַכָּתֵף עַמֻּדֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה. **Key terms:** כָּתֵף (katef) = "shoulder" — from כתף (katef, "shoulder"); the flanking section of the entrance. The east side has the courtyard gate in the center, with 15-cubit hangings on each side (vv. 14–15), plus a 20-cubit gate screen (v. 16), totaling 50 cubits. LXX: καὶ πεντεκαίδεκα πήχεων τῶν ἱστίων τὸ ὕψος τῷ κλίτει — the LXX uses ὕψος ("height") where the MT has length, a notable divergence.

**15** And for the second [ha-shenit] shoulder [ve-la-katef], fifteen [khamesh esreh] hangings [qela'im]; their pillars [ammudeihem] three [sheloshah], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] three [sheloshah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְלַכָּתֵף הַשֵּׁנִית חֲמֵשׁ עֶשְׂרֵה קְלָעִים עַמֻּדֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם שְׁלֹשָׁה. The second shoulder mirrors the first: 15 cubits of hangings flanking the other side of the gate, with three pillars and three sockets.

**16** And for the gate [u-le-sha'ar] of the courtyard [he-khatser], a screen [masakh] of twenty [esrim] cubits [ammah], of blue [tekhelet] and purple [ve-argaman] and scarlet yarn [ve-tola'at shani] and fine twisted linen [ve-shesh moshzar], the work [ma'aseh] of an embroiderer [roqem]; their pillars [ammudeihem] four [arba'ah], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] four [arba'ah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּלְשַׁעַר הֶחָצֵר מָסָךְ עֶשְׂרִים אַמָּה תְּכֵלֶת וְאַרְגָּמָן וְתוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי וְשֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר מַעֲשֵׂה רֹקֵם עַמֻּדֵיהֶם אַרְבָּעָה וְאַדְנֵיהֶם אַרְבָּעָה. **Key terms:** שַׁעַר (sha'ar) = "gate, opening" — the formal entrance to the courtyard on the east side. The gate screen is made of the same four materials (blue, purple, scarlet, fine linen) as the tabernacle entrance screen (26:36) and the parokhet (26:31), but like the entrance screen, it is ma'aseh roqem ("work of an embroiderer"), not ma'aseh khoshev ("work of a skilled craftsman"). The textile hierarchy applies to the boundary-points: embroidered gate screen (outermost) < embroidered entrance screen (middle) < woven veil with cherubim (innermost). LXX: καὶ τῇ πύλῃ τῆς αὐλῆς κάλυμμα εἴκοσι πήχεων τὸ ὕψος ἐξ ὑακίνθου... ἔργον ποικιλτοῦ — πύλη ("gate") for sha'ar.

**17** All [kol] the pillars [ammudei] of the courtyard [he-khatser] all around [saviv] shall be banded [mekhushshaqim] with silver [khesef]; their hooks [vaveihem] of silver [khesef], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] of bronze [nekhoshet].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כָּל־עַמּוּדֵי הֶחָצֵר סָבִיב מְחֻשָּׁקִים כֶּסֶף וָוֵיהֶם כֶּסֶף וְאַדְנֵיהֶם נְחֹשֶׁת. **Key terms:** מְחֻשָּׁקִים (mekhushshaqim) = pual participle of חשׁק (khashaq, "to bind, band") — "banded, filleted"; the pillars are encircled with silver bands. The courtyard pillar composition summarized: bronze sockets at the base, silver bands and hooks on the pillars — silver above, bronze below — maintaining the material-hierarchy principle at a lower register than the tabernacle interior. LXX: πάντες οἱ στῦλοι τῆς αὐλῆς κύκλῳ κατηργυρωμένοι ἀργυρίῳ — κατηργυρωμένος ("silver-plated") for mekhushshaqim.

**18** The length [orekh] of the courtyard [he-khatser] shall be a hundred [me'ah] cubits [ba-ammah], and the width [ve-rokhav] fifty [khamishim] by fifty [ba-khamishim], and the height [ve-qomah] five [khamesh] cubits [ammot] of fine twisted linen [shesh moshzar], and their sockets [ve-adneihem] of bronze [nekhoshet].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אֹרֶךְ הֶחָצֵר מֵאָה בָאַמָּה וְרֹחַב חֲמִשִּׁים בַּחֲמִשִּׁים וְקֹמָה חָמֵשׁ אַמּוֹת שֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר וְאַדְנֵיהֶם נְחֹשֶׁת. **Key terms:** חֲמִשִּׁים בַּחֲמִשִּׁים (khamishim ba-khamishim) = "fifty by fifty" — an unusual expression, perhaps meaning "fifty [at each end], consistently fifty" or "fifty on each side." The courtyard dimensions: 100 cubits long (north and south), 50 cubits wide (east and west), 5 cubits high — the hangings are 5 cubits tall (approximately 7.5 feet), creating a visual barrier but not a massive wall. LXX: τὸ δὲ μῆκος τῆς αὐλῆς ἑκατὸν ἐφ᾽ ἑκατόν — "a hundred by a hundred" — notable divergence from MT's "fifty by fifty."

**19** For all [le-khol] the vessels [kelei] of the tabernacle [ha-mishkan] in all [be-khol] its service [avodato], and all [ve-khol] its pegs [yetedotav], and all [ve-khol] the pegs [yitdot] of the courtyard [he-khatser] — bronze [nekhoshet].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לְכֹל כְּלֵי הַמִּשְׁכָּן בְּכֹל עֲבֹדָתוֹ וְכָל־יְתֵדֹתָיו וְכָל־יִתְדֹת הֶחָצֵר נְחֹשֶׁת. **Key terms:** יְתֵדֹת (yetedot) = "pegs, stakes, tent-pins" — from יתד (yated, "peg, pin"); the ground stakes that anchor the tabernacle's ropes and the courtyard hangings. עֲבֹדָה (avodah) = "service, work" — from עבד (avad, "to serve, work"); all functional implements and structural hardware are bronze. This verse serves as a summary statement: everything in the outer precinct — the utilitarian hardware and the structural fasteners — is bronze. LXX: καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐργαλεῖα καὶ πάντες οἱ πάσσαλοι τῆς αὐλῆς χαλκοῖ — πάσσαλος ("peg") for yated; ἐργαλεῖον ("tool, implement") for keli.

---

## The Oil for the Lamp (vv. 20–21)

**20** And you [ve-attah] shall command [tetsavveh] the sons of Israel [et-benei Yisrael], and they shall take [ve-yiqkhu] to you [eilekha] oil [shemen] of olives [zayit], pure [zakh], beaten [katit], for the light [la-ma'or], to cause a lamp to go up [le-ha'alot ner] continually [tamid].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאַתָּה תְּצַוֶּה אֶת־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְיִקְחוּ אֵלֶיךָ שֶׁמֶן זַיִת זָךְ כָּתִית לַמָּאוֹר לְהַעֲלֹת נֵר תָּמִיד. **Key terms:** וְאַתָּה (ve-attah) = "and you" — emphatic pronoun, placing personal responsibility on Moses. תְּצַוֶּה (tetsavveh) = piel imperfect of צוה (tsavah, "to command") — "you shall command"; this is the root of the chapter name in the traditional parashah division: Tetsavveh (Exod 27:20–30:10). שֶׁמֶן זַיִת זָךְ כָּתִית (shemen zayit zakh katit) = "oil of olive, pure, beaten" — zakh = "pure, clear" (free of sediment); katit = "beaten, pressed" (from כתת, katat, "to beat, crush") — the finest grade of olive oil, produced by crushing olives in a mortar rather than pressing in an oil press, yielding a clearer, purer oil for the lampstand. לְהַעֲלֹת נֵר (le-ha'alot ner) = "to cause a lamp to go up" — hiphil infinitive construct of עלה (alah, "to go up"); lighting the lamp is described as "causing it to ascend," reflecting the flame's upward movement. תָּמִיד (tamid) = "continually, perpetually" — the lamp is to burn as an ongoing obligation, not merely during the construction phase. This is the first perpetual liturgical command in the tabernacle instructions. LXX: καὶ σὺ σύνταξον τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ καὶ λαβέτωσάν σοι ἔλαιον ἐξ ἐλαιῶν ἄτρυγον καθαρὸν κεκομμένον εἰς φῶς καῦσαι λύχνον διὰ παντός — ἄτρυγον ("without dregs") for zakh; κεκομμένον ("beaten") for katit; λύχνος ("lamp") for ner.

**21** In the tent [be-ohel] of meeting [mo'ed], outside [mi-khuts] the veil [la-parokhet] which [asher] is before [al] the testimony [ha-edut], Aaron [Aharon] and his sons [u-vanav] shall arrange [ya'arokh] it [oto] from evening [me-erev] until [ad] morning [boqer] before [lifnei] YHWH — a statute [khuqqat] of perpetuity [olam] for their generations [le-dorotam] from [me-et] the sons of Israel [benei Yisrael].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads בְּאֹהֶל מוֹעֵד מִחוּץ לַפָּרֹכֶת אֲשֶׁר עַל־הָעֵדוּת יַעֲרֹךְ אֹתוֹ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו מֵעֶרֶב עַד־בֹּקֶר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה חֻקַּת עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתָם מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל. **Key terms:** אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (ohel mo'ed) = "tent of meeting" — the first use of this full designation in the tabernacle instructions; mo'ed (from יעד, ya'ad, "to appoint, meet") = "appointed meeting, designated time/place." יַעֲרֹךְ (ya'arokh) = qal imperfect of ערך (arakh, "to arrange, set in order") — "he shall arrange"; Aaron's priestly duty of tending the lamp. מֵעֶרֶב עַד־בֹּקֶר (me-erev ad-boqer) = "from evening until morning" — the lamp burns through the night hours. חֻקַּת עוֹלָם (khuqqat olam) = "a statute of perpetuity" — from חק (khoq, "statute, decree") + עולם (olam, "perpetuity, age"); a binding regulation for all future generations. לְדֹרֹתָם מֵאֵת בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (le-dorotam me-et benei Yisrael) = "for their generations, from the sons of Israel" — the oil is the community's contribution; the tending is the priesthood's duty. LXX: ἐν τῇ σκηνῇ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ἔξωθεν τοῦ καταπετάσματος τοῦ ἐπὶ τῆς διαθήκης καύσει αὐτὸ Ααρων καὶ οἱ υἱοὶ αὐτοῦ ἀφ᾽ ἑσπέρας ἕως πρωὶ ἐναντίον κυρίου νόμιμον αἰώνιον εἰς τὰς γενεὰς ὑμῶν παρὰ τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ — σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("tent of testimony") for ohel mo'ed; νόμιμον αἰώνιον ("eternal ordinance") for khuqqat olam; κύριος ("Lord") for YHWH.

---

## Translator's Afterword

Exodus 27 completes the outward movement begun in chapter 25. Where chapter 25 described the innermost furnishings (ark, table, lampstand) and chapter 26 the tabernacle structure itself, chapter 27 addresses the outer precinct — the bronze altar, the courtyard enclosure, and the perpetual lamp oil. The architectural logic radiates concentrically from YHWH's throne outward to the boundary between sacred and common space.

The bronze altar (vv. 1–8) is the dominant feature of the courtyard and the largest piece of tabernacle furniture. Its 5 x 5 cubit footprint dwarfs the ark (2.5 x 1.5 cubits) and the table (2 x 1 cubit). The altar is entirely bronze-overlaid — the first object described without any gold. The material transition is now complete: gold dominates the Holy of Holies and Holy Place; bronze governs the courtyard. The altar's horns (qarnotav, v. 2) are specified as "from it" (mimmenu) — organically one with the structure, not applied ornament — paralleling the cherubim beaten "from" the kapporet (25:19) and the branches "from" the lampstand (25:33). The Hebrew insists on integral unity at every level of the sacred architecture.

The courtyard (vv. 9–19) establishes the final boundary. At 100 x 50 cubits, it is the largest defined space in the complex. The linen hangings stand only 5 cubits high — tall enough to screen the interior from casual view, but not a fortress wall. The east side is divided into three sections (15 + 20 + 15 cubits), with the central 20-cubit gate screen employing the same four colored materials as the inner textiles. The summary of materials in vv. 17–19 confirms the hierarchy: silver bands and hooks on the pillars, bronze sockets below, bronze pegs throughout. Every structural element in the courtyard is bronze — the metal of the threshold between sacred and profane.

The oil commandment (vv. 20–21) shifts abruptly from architecture to liturgical practice. The emphatic ve-attah ("and you") addresses Moses directly and personally. The oil must be shemen zayit zakh katit — "oil of olive, pure, beaten" — the finest grade, hand-crushed rather than press-extracted. The lamp is to burn tamid ("continually"), tended by Aaron and his sons "from evening until morning." This is the first perpetual obligation in the tabernacle instructions, and it introduces Aaron and the priesthood into the construction narrative for the first time. The chapter thus bridges from sacred architecture to sacred practice — from building the space to inhabiting it with ongoing worship. The designation ohel mo'ed ("tent of meeting") appears here for the first time in the instruction sequence, naming the tabernacle by its relational function: not merely a dwelling (mishkan) but a place of appointed encounter between YHWH and Israel.

Key MT-LXX divergences: (1) In v. 3, the LXX introduces a στεφάνη ("rim, crown") for the altar before the utensils list, corresponding to the karkob of v. 5 but relocated; the MT's utensil order differs from the LXX's arrangement. (2) In v. 14, the LXX renders the hangings measurement with ὕψος ("height") rather than the MT's length, a persistent interpretive difference in the courtyard dimensions. (3) In v. 18, the LXX reads "a hundred by a hundred" (ἑκατὸν ἐφ᾽ ἑκατόν) where the MT has "fifty by fifty" (khamishim ba-khamishim) — a significant numerical divergence. (4) In v. 21, the LXX renders ohel mo'ed as σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("tent of testimony") rather than "tent of meeting," interpreting mo'ed through the lens of edut ("testimony") and aligning it with the ark of the testimony. (5) Throughout, the LXX maintains its characteristic rendering of shittim as asepta ("incorruptible") and nekhoshet as chalkos ("bronze/copper"). (6) In v. 21, the LXX uses κύριος ("Lord") for the Tetragrammaton YHWH, following the standard Septuagintal convention of substituting the divine title for the divine name.
